title: Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to [national estimates](#dod:ilo-national-estimates) and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of workers in informal employment vs. GDP per capita
Share of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection and employment benefits. Does not include illegal and illicit activities. Figures refer to national estimates and may not be fully comparable across countries. GDP per capita is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

This scatter plot maps the relationship between national economic output and labor market conditions, plotting the share of workers in informal employment against GDP per capita for 2024. The vertical axis measures the percentage of employed people working in jobs that lack basic social or legal protection, ranging from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices on a logarithmic scale from $2,000 to $100,000. Circle size corresponds to population, with India appearing as the largest circle due to its demographic scale. The data reveals a strong negative association: countries with lower GDP per capita consistently show higher shares of informal employment. In the upper-left quadrant, nations such as the Democratic Republic of Congo, Chad, Tanzania, and Somalia cluster with informal employment shares between 80% and 90% while maintaining GDP per capita below $3,000. Moving toward the center, countries like India, Pakistan, Ghana, and Indonesia occupy the middle range with informal shares between 60% and 85% and GDP per capita between $5,000 and $15,000. Further right, emerging economies including Mexico, Argentina, Brazil, and Turkey show informal employment shares declining to the 20% to 50% range as GDP per capita rises to $20,000 to $40,000. At the far right, high-income nations such as Germany, Spain, South Korea, and Russia cluster near the bottom axis with informal employment shares below 20% and GDP per capita exceeding $40,000. Regional coloring distinguishes continents, with African nations (purple) dominating the high-informality, low-income zone and European nations (blue) concentrated in the low-informality, high-income zone. The distribution shows that higher GDP per capita coincides with lower shares of informal employment across the visible range.
